programs.name: Medical Assistance
programs.tagline: Pennsylvania's Medicaid program can cover all medical costs for aging parents with limited income and assets.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ with limited income and assets, Pennsylvania's Medical Assistance (Medicaid) can cover their entire medical costs — doctor visits, hospital stays, prescription drugs, and long-term care including nursing homes. For nursing home coverage, your parent can have up to $2,982/month in income (2026) and still qualify.

Because Medical Assistance covers comprehensive health services under one program, your parent won't need to worry about medical bills, prescription costs, or coordinating between insurance plans. The program also covers home and community-based services that can help your parent stay in their home longer.

The application process is lengthy and asset limits are strict — $2,000 for individuals, $3,000 for couples — but the program can save thousands per month in medical expenses.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: For long-term care: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (clinical assessment showing need for daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management)
otherRequirements: Pennsylvania residency with intent to stay

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at COMPASS or call 1-866-550-4355 — processing takes several weeks to months depending on case complexity.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect identity proof, income statements, asset documentation, and medical records if applying for long-term care

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at COMPASS.state.pa.us, call 1-866-550-4355, or visit your local County Assistance Office

steps.title: Complete eligibility interview
steps.description: County staff will schedule an interview to review your parent's application and documentation

steps.title: Await medical assessment
steps.description: If applying for long-term care, your parent will need a clinical assessment to determine Nursing Facility Level of Care

applicationGuide.processingTime: Several weeks to months depending on case complexity and documentation
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible waitlist for home and community-based services due to funding limits
applicationGuide.tip: Start gathering financial documents early — the program requires detailed asset and income verification going back several months.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Life insurance with face value over $1,500
excluded: Primary home (if your parent intends to return)
Burial plots and reasonable burial funds

tiers.name: Standard Medical Assistance
tiers.description: Basic health coverage for adults 65+ or with disabilities
tiers.coverage: Doctor visits, hospital care, prescription drugs, lab tests, preventive services

tiers.name: Nursing Home Medicaid
tiers.description: Long-term care in skilled nursing facilities
tiers.coverage: All nursing home costs plus medical care

tiers.name: Home & Community Services
tiers.description: Services to help your parent stay at home
tiers.coverage: Personal care, home modifications, adult day programs (waitlist possible)

categories.name: Identity
items: Proof of citizenship or immigration status

contentSections.text: Home and community-based services may have waitlists due to funding limits. Apply early if your parent needs these services to remain at home.
If your parent's income or assets are slightly over the limits, consider Medicaid planning strategies. Some asset transfers or income arrangements may help them qualify legally.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for Medical Assistance?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is exempt from asset limits as long as your mom intends to return home or the equity is under certain limits. However, the state may place a lien to recover costs after death.

faqs.question: What if Dad's Social Security puts him just over the income limit?
faqs.answer: Income limits vary by program type and there may be deductions available. Call 1-866-550-4355 to discuss his specific situation — sometimes medical expenses can reduce countable income.

faqs.question: Does my parent's spouse's income count toward the limits?
faqs.answer: If they live together, yes — spousal income is counted. However, special protections exist for the community spouse (the one not needing care) to keep some income and assets.

faqs.question: How long does it take to get approved for nursing home coverage?
faqs.answer: The process can take several weeks to months. Your parent needs both financial eligibility approval and a clinical assessment showing they meet Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements.

faqs.question: Will Medical Assistance cover Mom's current doctors?
faqs.answer: Medical Assistance works with many providers, but not all doctors accept Medicaid. Check with her current doctors about acceptance before applying, or be prepared to find new providers.

faqs.question: What happens if we don't report a change in income or assets?
faqs.answer: You must report changes within 10 days. Failure to report can result in overpayments that must be repaid, loss of benefits, or fraud charges. Annual renewals also verify continued eligibility.

programs.name: Community HealthChoices
programs.tagline: Medicaid-covered home care services to help your parent avoid nursing home placement while staying in their own home.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management but wants to stay home, Community HealthChoices can provide up to 32 different services through Medicaid. Because this program covers nursing facility-level care at home, your parent can get attendant care, home modifications, and medical support without moving to a nursing home.

Your parent must be enrolled in Medicaid and meet nursing facility level of care requirements (needing help with basic daily activities). Income limit: $2,901/month per person in 2025, evaluated individually even for married couples. The program operates statewide in Pennsylvania through local managed care organizations.

exemptAssets: Primary home up to $730,000 equity if living there or intend to return

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must qualify for nursing facility level of care through a Functional Eligibility Determination (clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, or other Activities of Daily Living)
otherRequirements: Dual eligible (Medicare and Medicaid) OR qualify for Medicaid long-term services

applicationGuide.summary: Call Pennsylvania Independent Enrollment Broker at 1-877-550-4227 or apply online through COMPASS; includes financial review and in-person needs assessment within 90 days.

steps.title: Submit financial application
steps.description: Complete PA-600 form through COMPASS website or submit to your County Assistance Office to establish Medicaid eligibility.

steps.title: Request services assessment
steps.description: Call Pennsylvania Independent Enrollment Broker at 1-877-550-4227 to request Community HealthChoices enrollment.

steps.title: Complete needs assessment
steps.description: Local Area Agency on Aging will conduct in-person assessment to determine nursing facility level of care and specific service needs.

steps.title: Choose managed care plan
steps.description: Select from available Managed Care Organizations in your region after approval to coordinate services.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Screening within 90 days for new dual eligibles; comprehensive needs assessment if nursing facility level of care is needed
applicationGuide.tip: If your parent is 55+ and in a LIFE program service area, compare LIFE benefits as an alternative before choosing Community HealthChoices.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
excluded: Primary home (if equity under $730,000 and you live there or intend to return)
Home if non-applicant spouse or dependent child lives there

categories.name: Medical
items: Medical records showing need for daily care assistance

contentSections.text: Income and assets are evaluated individually for each spouse, even if married. Each person can have up to $2,901/month in income and still qualify separately.

faqs.question: Can my parent keep their house if they qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes, if your parent lives in the home or intends to return, and home equity is under $730,000. The home is also exempt if a non-applicant spouse or dependent child lives there.

faqs.question: What if my parent has both Medicare and Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Your parent can still get Community HealthChoices services. Medicare benefits continue separately - Community HealthChoices adds long-term care services that Medicare doesn't cover.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Community HealthChoices and LIFE?
faqs.answer: Both provide home-based care, but LIFE is only available to people 55+ in certain service areas and includes medical care. Community HealthChoices is statewide for ages 21+ but focuses on long-term care services while Medicare handles medical care separately.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent's income is just over $2,901/month?
faqs.answer: They won't qualify for Community HealthChoices. However, if they're married, each spouse is evaluated individually - so one spouse might qualify even if the other's income is too high.

faqs.question: How long does the needs assessment take?
faqs.answer: The Area Agency on Aging will schedule an in-person assessment after financial eligibility is confirmed. This assessment determines what level of care your parent needs and which specific services they'll receive.

faqs.question: Can my parent choose their care providers?
faqs.answer: After approval, your parent must choose a Managed Care Organization (MCO) in their region. The MCO then coordinates all services and may have a network of providers to choose from.

programs.name: PACE (Pharmaceutical Assistance Contract for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: Pennsylvania prescription drug coverage with $6-$9 co-pays for parents 65+ on limited income.
programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and lives on Social Security or a small pension, PACE can dramatically reduce their prescription costs. The program pays for prescription drugs with low co-payments: $6 for generics, $9 for brand-name medications. No monthly premium.

PACE serves Pennsylvania residents with modest incomes — single people earning up to $14,500/year, married couples up to $17,700/year (based on previous year's income). Because the program doesn't count assets like savings or home value, your parent can qualify even if they own their home or have money in the bank. The only requirement: they can't already have Medicaid prescription coverage.
programs.savingsSource: Based on typical prescription usage with $6-$9 co-pays vs. retail prices

otherRequirements: Pennsylvania resident for at least 90 consecutive days

applicationGuide.summary: Apply by calling 1-800-225-7223 or online — processing time not specified by state.

steps.title: Gather income documents
steps.description: Collect previous year's Social Security statements, pension statements, tax returns, or pay stubs for both spouses if married.

steps.title: Prove Pennsylvania residency
steps.description: Have documentation showing your parent lived in Pennsylvania for at least 90 consecutive days.

steps.title: Apply by phone or online
steps.description: Call 1-800-225-7223 or visit pacecares.magellanhealth.com to submit application.

steps.title: Get local help if needed
steps.description: Call PA MEDI helpline at 1-800-783-7067 for assistance completing the application.

applicationGuide.tip: Income is based on the previous calendar year, so if your parent's income dropped in 2024, they may qualify for 2025 coverage even if they didn't qualify before.

contentSections.heading: Income: What Counts
included: Gross Social Security and SSI (before Medicare premium deductions)

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of age (birth certificate or driver's license)

categories.name: Income Verification
items: Spouse's income documents if married and living together

contentSections.text: Don't confuse this with the national PACE program for nursing home-level care. Pennsylvania's PACE is prescription drug assistance only.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is slightly over the PACE limit?
faqs.answer: They may qualify for PACENET, the companion program for higher incomes. Single people earning $14,501–$33,500 or couples earning $17,701–$41,500 can get coverage with higher co-pays ($8 generic, $15 brand-name) and a monthly premium of about $37.45.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house and savings if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE has no asset limits. Your parent can own their home, have money in savings, or other assets and still qualify. Only income matters for eligibility.

faqs.question: What if Dad has Medicare but not Medicaid prescription coverage?
faqs.answer: He can likely qualify for PACE. The program excludes people with Medicaid prescription benefits, but Medicare alone doesn't disqualify him. Even Medicare Savings Program participants (who get help with Part B premiums) can still qualify.

faqs.question: Does PACE cover all prescription drugs?
faqs.answer: PACE covers most prescription medications, but specific coverage details aren't provided in state sources. Call 1-800-225-7223 to verify coverage for your parent's specific medications before applying.

faqs.question: My parent moved to Pennsylvania recently — can they apply right away?
faqs.answer: They must wait until they've been a Pennsylvania resident for 90 consecutive days before applying. Mark this date on your calendar and apply immediately after the 90-day period.

faqs.question: When does the income counting period change for next year's eligibility?
faqs.answer: Income limits are based on the previous calendar year. For 2025 coverage, they'll look at 2024 income. If your parent's income dropped significantly in 2024, they may qualify even if they didn't qualify for 2024 coverage.
